name: HTTP 301 Moved Permanently
content: A 301 response tells the user agent that the requested resource has been permanently moved to a new URI. Conforming browsers cache the redirect and go directly to the target on subsequent requests, eliminating a round-trip to the origin server. Side-effect: the origin server never sees repeat visits, so click analytics become unreliable.
quote: The 301 (Moved Permanently) status code indicates that the target resource has been assigned a new permanent URI and any future references to this resource ought to use one of the enclosed URIs. — RFC 7231 §6.4.2

name: HTTP 302 Found (Temporary Redirect)
content: A 302 response indicates a temporary redirect. The user agent re-requests the original URI on every visit, so the origin server receives each request and can count clicks. Preferred when click analytics or A/B testing is required. Trade-off: one extra network round-trip per redirect versus 301.
quote: The 302 (Found) status code indicates that the target resource resides temporarily under a different URI. Since the redirection might be altered on occasion, the client ought to continue using the effective request URI for future requests. — RFC 7231 §6.4.3

name: Base62 Encoding for Short Codes
content: Base62 uses the 62 characters [0-9a-zA-Z] to represent integers as compact, URL-safe strings. A 6-character base62 code covers 62^6 ≈ 56.8 billion unique values — enough for decades of URL shortener operation at 100 M new URLs per day. Compared to base64, base62 avoids the '+' and '/' characters that require percent-encoding in URLs.
source: General computer science knowledge; no single authoritative source

name: Key Generation Service (KGS) Pattern
content: A KGS pre-generates unique IDs and hands out batches to API servers. Each API server holds a local batch (e.g. 1 000 IDs) and assigns them without contacting the KGS on every request. This eliminates the KGS as a per-request bottleneck. The KGS itself is kept simple (two instances: active + standby) and its Key DB stores only a monotonically advancing counter range. Crash recovery: the standby takes over; any unused IDs in a crashed API server's batch are simply skipped (small waste, no correctness issue).
source: Common industry pattern; described in multiple public system-design resources

name: Read-Heavy System Optimisation Order
content: For systems with a high read-to-write ratio (100:1 or more), the correct optimisation sequence is: (1) add in-memory caching first — highest ROI, cheapest infrastructure; (2) add read replicas to protect the primary DB write path; (3) consider sharding only when a single primary's write throughput is the bottleneck. Jumping straight to sharding for a read problem is a common interview mistake.

name: Stateless API Server Design
content: An API server is stateless when each request contains all information needed to process it and the server holds no per-session state between requests. Stateless servers can be added or removed freely behind a load balancer with no coordination overhead. For a URL shortener, the only state lives in the DB and cache — the API layer is intentionally kept stateless to enable horizontal scaling.

name: Cache Warming vs Lazy Population
content: Two strategies for populating a cache. Lazy population (cache-aside): on a cache miss, the application fetches from the DB, writes the result to the cache, and returns it. The cache fills naturally with whatever is actually accessed. Eager warming: proactively load the hottest N keys at startup. For a URL shortener, lazy population suffices for most codes; eager warming can be applied to the top-1000 hottest short codes (e.g. a viral link) to prevent a stampede on startup or after cache flush.
source: General caching literature; see also Redis documentation on cache patterns

name: URL Shortener — Core Design Decision Matrix
content: The three decisions that determine the URL shortener's architecture: (1) ID generation — hash-and-truncate (stateless, collision-prone at scale) vs counter+KGS (collision-free, requires KGS HA); (2) redirect type — 302 for analytics accuracy vs 301 for client-side caching and load reduction; (3) caching scope — in-process LRU for top-N hottest codes + distributed Redis for the long tail. All three are independent axes and should be justified against the stated non-functional requirements.
source: Synthesised from public system-design interview guides and RFC 7231
